headline: Visual Execution Trumps Strategic Vision
body:
March delivered a stark bifurcation in creative demand that challenges conventional wisdom about design hierarchy. While Brand & Visual Design surged 22.4% year-over-year, Design Leadership flat-lined, suggesting organizations are prioritizing tactical execution over strategic oversight.

The month's defining story lies in Visual Designer demand, which exploded 161.5% despite carrying a 64% AI risk rating. This surge occurred alongside a dramatic -42.3% collapse in Copywriter positions, creating the widest demand gap between visual and written content roles in our index's history. The pattern suggests companies are betting on human visual judgment while increasingly trusting AI for written content—a calculated risk given Copywriters' 69% AI risk score, the highest across all tracked roles.

Design Leadership faced an existential reckoning in March. Creative Director positions contracted -26.7%, while Design Director roles gained 26.7%—a perfect inverse that signals organizational restructuring toward operational rather than conceptual leadership. This 53.4 percentage point swing between similar roles suggests companies are consolidating strategic functions while expanding tactical oversight. The trend aligns with both roles' relatively low AI risk scores (19% and 18% respectively), indicating the decline stems from structural changes rather than automation pressure.

Product & UX Design revealed internal stratification that belies its modest 2.6% cluster growth. UX Researcher demand jumped 41.7%, while UX Designer positions fell -35.3%—a 77 percentage point divergence within the same discipline. This split reflects organizations doubling down on research-driven insights while questioning the execution layer previously considered foundational to user experience. The pattern is particularly notable given UX Researchers' low 27% AI risk score compared to UX Designers' moderate 35% rating.

The salary stagnation across all clusters—every role showed 0% year-over-year compensation growth—compounds these demand shifts into a liquidity crisis for creative talent. With 194,354 total openings in the market but six roles in active decline, the creative economy is reshaping around a smaller set of highly valued functions while leaving traditional roles behind. Organizations appear to be parsing creative work into irreplaceable human judgment and replaceable routine execution, with March's data suggesting they've made their initial cuts.
